Karpathy provides a comprehensive introduction to large language models, explaining that they are fundamentally neural networks trained to predict the next word in a sequence, composed of just two files (parameters and runtime code). He details the two-stage training process (pre-training on internet text and fine-tuning on curated Q&A data), discusses scaling laws that predictably improve performance with more parameters and data, and demonstrates concrete capabilities including tool use, multimodality, and extended problem-solving. He concludes by framing LLMs as emerging operating system kernels coordinating computational resources and tools, identifying key future research directions including System 2 reasoning, self-improvement mechanisms, and task-specific customization.

directClaims: A large language model is fundamentally just two files: a parameters file and a runtime file that executes those parameters, Llama 2 70B model has 140 gigabytes of parameters stored as float16 numbers (2 bytes each), Training Llama 2 70B requires roughly 10 terabytes of text, 6,000 GPUs, 12 days of compute, and costs about $2 million, Model training is lossy compression of internet text, similar to a zip file but with information loss, Large language models perform next-word prediction as their fundamental objective, Pre-training uses large quantities of internet text (potentially low quality), while fine-tuning uses smaller quantities of high-quality curated Q&A data, Fine-tuning to create an assistant model typically uses 100,000 high-quality documents created by human labelers, Scaling laws show that model performance is a predictable function of two variables: number of parameters (N) and amount of training text (D), Current state-of-the-art models (GPT-4, Claude, Bard) require training budgets 10x or more larger than Llama 2 70B, Proprietary models (GPT, Claude) currently outperform open-source models but are only accessible via web interfaces, Large language models lack System 2 thinking and cannot currently perform extended reasoning or tree-of-thought search, AlphaGo surpassed human players through self-play rather than imitation learning alone, Tool use including web browsing, code execution, image generation, and mathematical calculation increasingly enable LLM capability, Multimodality (text, image, audio, video) is a major axis of improvement for large language models

inferredBeliefs: The author believes LLMs should be understood as operating system kernels orchestrating resources rather than simple chatbots, The field will increasingly focus on extending model reasoning through System 2 capabilities and longer inference-time computation, Self-improvement beyond human performance is a key frontier, though currently limited by lack of clear reward functions in open-ended domains, The LLM ecosystem will follow patterns similar to historical operating systems: proprietary systems competing with open-source alternatives, Tool use and multimodality are essential for practical capability, not peripheral features, Current understanding of how neural network parameters collaborate is fundamentally limited despite knowing the architecture, Scaling (larger models, more data) will continue to drive capability improvements without necessarily requiring algorithmic breakthroughs, Fine-tuning and customization will become increasingly important for domain-specific applications

mentionedTools: Llama 2, ChatGPT, GPT-4, Claude, Bard, Zephyr 7B, Mistral, C programming language, Python, Bing search, DALL-E, Matplotlib, OpenAI, Anthropic, Deep Mind, AlphaGo, Linux, Windows, MacOS, Chatbot Arena, Scale AI

topics: Training pipeline: collect internet text → procure GPU cluster → train model → obtain parameters file and runtime code, Fine-tuning pipeline: write labeling instructions → hire labelers → create Q&A examples → fine-tune base model → evaluate → deploy → collect failures → fix failures iteratively, Multi-stage improvement: Stage 1 (pre-training on internet text) → Stage 2 (fine-tuning on Q&A data) → Stage 3 (optional: training with comparison labels via RLHF), Tool-use workflow: LLM identifies need for tool → emits special tokens → external tool executes (search, calculator, code interpreter, image generation) → results fed back to LLM → LLM generates response, Customization through retrieval: upload custom documents → LLM references chunks from documents via retrieval augmented generation → LLM generates contextualized responses, Iterative problem-solving: ask question → LLM uses tools to research → organize findings → generate visualization → extrapolate results → generate images based on analysis

Andrej Karpathy provides a comprehensive deep dive into tokenization in large language models, explaining why it matters, how it works at a fundamental level, and walking through a complete implementation of Byte Pair Encoding from scratch. He demonstrates how tokenization directly impacts LLM capabilities across languages, code, arithmetic, and other domains through interactive examples and live code walkthroughs.

directClaims: Tokenization is the process for translating strings or text into sequences of tokens and vice versa, GPT-2 uses a vocabulary of 50,257 possible tokens with a context size of 1,024 tokens, The Llama 2 paper mentions 'tokens' 63 times and states they trained on two trillion tokens of data, Unicode defines roughly 150,000 characters across 161 scripts as of Unicode 15.1 in September 2023, UTF-8 encoding takes every Unicode code point and translates it to a byte stream between one to four bytes, making it variable-length encoding, The Byte Pair Encoding algorithm iteratively finds the most frequently occurring pair of tokens and replaces it with a single new token, GPT-4 tokenizer has approximately 100,000 tokens, roughly double that of GPT-2's 50,000, GPT-4 tokenizer improved handling of whitespace for Python by grouping multiple spaces into single tokens, Non-English languages typically require more tokens than English for the same semantic content due to tokenizer training biases, A tokenizer is a completely separate preprocessing stage from the language model with its own training set, Strings in Python are immutable sequences of Unicode code points, Many issues in large language models trace back to tokenization, including spelling tasks, arithmetic, and non-English language performance

inferredBeliefs: Tokenization is complex and often overlooked despite being fundamental to LLM behavior, Understanding tokenization details is critical for debugging and optimizing LLM performance, The design choices in tokenizer architecture have significant downstream effects on model capabilities, There is room for innovation in tokenization approaches beyond Byte Pair Encoding, Tokenizer training data composition should be deliberately chosen based on target use cases and languages, UTF-8 is the correct encoding standard for modern systems despite some inefficiencies, Vocabulary size in tokenizers represents a tradeoff between sequence length and embedding table size

mentionedTools: Python, Google Colab, tiktoken (tokenizer tool), The Tokenizer web app (tiktokenizer.app), UTF-8 encoding, UTF-16 encoding, UTF-32 encoding, GPT-2 tokenizer, GPT-4 tokenizer (CL100K base), Unicode Consortium standard, Wikipedia, Block post/blog

topics: Encode raw text string to UTF-8 bytes to get initial token sequence, Use get_stats() function to count frequency of all consecutive token pairs in sequence, Identify the most frequently occurring pair in the token sequence, Mint a new token ID for that pair and add to vocabulary, Replace all occurrences of that pair with the new token ID throughout the sequence, Record the merge operation (child tokens → new token ID) in a merges dictionary, Repeat merging process iteratively until desired vocabulary size is reached, Build vocabulary mapping from token ID to byte representation using merges dictionary, For decoding: iterate through token IDs, look up byte representations, concatenate bytes, decode UTF-8 to string, For encoding: convert string to UTF-8 bytes, then iteratively apply merges in order until no more merges possible, Store tokenizer separately from language model as a preprocessing stage before training

Andrej implements a character-level language model (makemore) by progressively building from a simple multi-layer perceptron to a hierarchical WaveNet-inspired architecture that fuses information progressively rather than immediately concatenating all context. The video covers implementing core PyTorch modules from scratch, fixing subtle bugs in BatchNorm for 3D inputs, and improving validation loss from 2.10 to 1.99 through architectural changes and increased model capacity.

directClaims: We are implementing makemore, a character-level language model, The architecture takes multiple previous characters as input to predict the next character, We started with 3 characters of context and expanded to 8 characters, WaveNet is a paper published by DeepMind in 2016 that predicts audio sequences using similar autoregressive modeling, The dataset contains 182,000 examples of three characters predicting a fourth character, We developed layer modules including Linear, BatchNorm1d, and Tanh layers to mimic PyTorch APIs, BatchNorm has running mean and variance trained via exponential moving average during forward pass, BatchNorm behaves differently during training and evaluation, requiring careful state management, We implemented a Sequential container to organize layers into lists, Initial validation loss was 2.10, improved to 2.02 by increasing context length from 3 to 8, We implemented hierarchical progressive fusion of information rather than crushing all characters into a single layer, The hierarchical architecture uses consecutive flattening where pairs of characters are progressively fused through deeper layers, BatchNorm1d was incorrectly averaging only over batch dimension instead of batch and sequence dimensions for 3D inputs, The fix involved reducing over dimensions (0,1) instead of just (0) for 3D tensor inputs, After fixing BatchNorm, validation loss improved from 2.029 to 2.022, With increased embedding size (24) and hidden units, we achieved validation loss of 1.993 with 76,000 parameters, Convolutions are primarily used for efficiency in WaveNet implementations, not for changing the model itself, Convolutional approaches allow variable reuse and efficient sliding of filters over input sequences, PyTorch's BatchNorm1d expects channels in the middle dimension (N x C x L) while our implementation expects channels in the last dimension

inferredBeliefs: Building neural networks from first principles using basic operations deepens understanding of deep learning, Careful implementation of standardization layers is critical because they introduce state and mode-dependent behavior, Hierarchical architectures that progressively fuse information are more efficient than immediate full concatenation, Explicit dimension handling is preferable to implicit dimension resolution for catching errors, Model capacity should be measured by parameter count while architecture determines efficiency of parameter usage, Experimentation at scale requires systematic logging and tracking of hyperparameters and results, Understanding implementation details of standard libraries like PyTorch is valuable for debugging and optimization

mentionedTools: PyTorch (torch.nn, torch.tensor), matplotlib (plt.plot), Python, Kyoto (location mentioned for travel), CUDA kernels

topics: Read dataset and process into character-level examples, Create embedding table to convert character integers to vectors, Flatten consecutive embeddings using view operations, Pass through linear layers with batch normalization and activation functions, Monitor training and validation loss curves with smoothing, Sample from model by iteratively predicting next character given context, Inspect tensor shapes at each layer during debugging, Use break statements and temporary batch creation for debugging forward passes, Progressively increase model complexity and capacity through architecture changes, Set layers to eval mode before validation to disable training-specific behavior

metadata.summary: Andrej implements a character-level language model (makemore) by progressively building from a simple multi-layer perceptron to a hierarchical WaveNet-inspired architecture that fuses information progressively rather than immediately concatenating all context. The video covers implementing core PyTorch modules from scratch, fixing subtle bugs in BatchNorm for 3D inputs, and improving validation loss from 2.10 to 1.99 through architectural changes and increased model capacity.

backward pass manually on the level of tensors and I think that this is a very useful exercise for the following reasons I actually have an entire blog post on this topic but I'd like to call back propagation a leaky abstraction and what I mean by that is back propagation does doesn't just make your neural networks just work magically it's not the case they can just Stack Up arbitrary Lego blocks of differentiable functions and just cross your fingers and back propagate and everything is great things don't just work automatically it is a leaky abstraction in the sense that you can shoot yourself in the foot if you do not understanding its internals it will magically not work or not work optimally and you will need to understand how it works under the hood if you're hoping to debug it and if you are hoping to address it in your neural nut um so this blog post here from a while ago goes into some of those examples so for example we've already covered them some of them already for in a random code base on the internet where they actually have like a very subtle but pretty major bug in their implementation and the bug points at the fact that the author of this code does not actually understand by propagation so they're trying to do here is they're trying to clip the loss at a certain maximum value but actually what they're trying to do is they're trying to collect the gradients to have a maximum value instead of trying to clip the loss at a maximum value and um indirectly they're basically causing some of the outliers to be actually ignored because when you clip a loss of an outlier you are setting its gradient to zero and so have a look through this and read through it but there's basically a bunch of subtle issues that you're going to avoid if you actually covered autograd and we wrote micrograd but micrograd was an autograd engine only on the level of individual scalars so the atoms were single individual numbers and uh you know I don't think it's enough and I'd like us to basically think about back propagation on level of tensors as well and so in a summary I think it's a good exercise I think it is very very valuable you're going to become better at debugging neural networks and making sure that you understand what you're doing it is going to make everything fully explicit so you're not going to be nervous about what is hidden away from you and basically in general we're going to emerge stronger and so let's get into it a bit of a fun historical note here is that today writing your backward pass by hand and manually is not recommended and no one does it except for the purposes of exercise but about 10 years ago in deep learning this was fairly standard and in fact pervasive so at the time everyone used to write their Andrej Karpathy walks through implementing backpropagation manually at the tensor level instead of using PyTorch's autograd, demonstrating the complete forward and backward pass for a two-layer multilayer perceptron with batch normalization. He emphasizes that understanding backpropagation internals is crucial for debugging neural networks and uses small mathematical examples to derive correct gradient formulas, showing that dimensional analysis alone can guide the correct implementation without memorizing formulas.

directClaims: Back propagation is a leaky abstraction - you can shoot yourself in the foot if you don't understand its internals, Saturating activation functions cause gradients to die, Dead neurons are a known issue in neural networks, Exploding or vanishing gradients occur in recurrent neural networks, Writing backward passes by hand was standard practice about 10 years ago in deep learning, Today, writing your backward pass by hand manually is not recommended except for purposes of exercise, PyTorch's autograd engine calculates gradients automatically, removing the need for manual backward pass implementation, The max operation in softmax is used for numerical stability to prevent overflow, Changing the logit maximum does not change the softmax probabilities, Matrix multiplication backward pass can be derived from first principles by breaking down small examples, The derivative of loss with respect to logits in cross-entropy equals the negative mean of selected log probabilities, Dimensions must align correctly in matrix operations - there is only one way to make shapes work out, Broadcasting in PyTorch automatically replicates tensors to align dimensions

inferredBeliefs: Deep understanding of backpropagation internals is essential for effective neural network debugging, Pedagogical value comes from implementing concepts from scratch before using automatic tools, Mathematical reasoning on paper using small examples clarifies complex tensor operations, Explicit implementation with intermediate tensors aids understanding over collapsed abstract operations, Historical context and evolution of deep learning practices provides valuable perspective, Dimensional analysis is a powerful tool for deriving correct formulas without memorization, Gradient checking against automatic differentiation is a crucial validation technique

mentionedTools: PyTorch, make more, micrograd, Matlab, NumPy, Google Colab, GitHub, Jupyter Notebook, torch.tensor, torch.allclose, F.one_hot, F.cross_entropy

topics: Forward pass: compute intermediate tensors with retain_grad() for all values needed in backward, Initialize network with small random numbers instead of zeros to avoid masking gradient errors, Implement backward pass manually by computing d-variables in reverse topological order, Use gradient checking to compare manually computed gradients against PyTorch's autograd, Derive local derivatives for each operation mathematically, Apply chain rule by multiplying local derivatives with upstream gradients, Handle broadcasting by summing gradients across replicated dimensions with keepdim=True, Use dimensional analysis to determine correct matrix multiplication order, Validate intermediate results using comparison function before proceeding

This video walks through reproducing the GPT-2 124 million parameter model from scratch. Karpathy loads the official OpenAI weights using Hugging Face Transformers, implements a custom PyTorch GPT-2 class to match the architecture, and generates text to verify correctness before moving to training from random initialization on the tiny Shakespeare dataset.

directClaims: GPT-2 was released by OpenAI in 2019 with a blog post, paper, and GitHub code, The 124 million parameter model is the smallest in the GPT-2 miniseries, ranging from 124M to 1.5B parameters, GPT-2 124M has 12 layers in the Transformer with 768 channels/dimensions, GPT-2 uses a decoder-only Transformer architecture, not an encoder-decoder like the original Transformer, GPT-2 applies layer normalization before attention and feed-forward (pre-normalization), unlike the original Transformer which applies it after, An additional layer normalization was added before the final classifier in GPT-2, GPT-2 uses the approximate GELU nonlinearity (10h) rather than the exact version, GPT-2 uses multi-headed attention with queries, keys, and values, The GPT-2 vocabulary size is 50,257 tokens, GPT-2 has a maximum sequence length of 1,024 tokens, The positional embeddings in GPT-2 are learned parameters, not fixed sinusoids like in the original Transformer, You can reproduce the GPT-2 124M model in roughly one hour on cloud compute for about $10, OpenAI released the weights for GPT-2 publicly, The original GPT-2 code was written in TensorFlow, Attention is a communication/aggregation operation where tokens exchange information, MLP operates individually on each token with no information exchange between tokens, The Transformer is a repeated application of map and reduce operations, Hugging Face Transformers library converted GPT-2 weights from TensorFlow to PyTorch, Top-K sampling with K=50 is used in the default Hugging Face pipeline for generation

inferredBeliefs: Prefers PyTorch over TensorFlow for modern deep learning work, Values interpretability and understanding of model internals over using black-box implementations, Believes in building models from scratch to gain full understanding rather than relying on library abstractions, Sees historical quirks in ML (like approximate GELU) as interesting artifacts worth understanding, Thinks clean residual pathways are important for optimization and gradient flow, Believes GPU acceleration is essential for practical neural network training, Prefers minimal, readable implementations over feature-complete library code, Values reproducibility and ability to verify results against official releases, Thinks step-by-step visualization of learned parameters (like positional embeddings) is scientifically interesting, Believes mechanistic interpretability of neural networks is worth exploring

mentionedTools: PyTorch, TensorFlow, Hugging Face Transformers, Jupyter Notebook, VS Code, Jupyter Notebook extension for VS Code, Tick Tokenizer (OpenAI's tokenizer library), CUDA, MPS (Apple Metal Performance Shaders), GitHub, tiny Shakespeare dataset, torch.compile

Andrej Karpathy builds a character-level language model from scratch using PyTorch, starting with a simple Bigram model and progressively introducing the Transformer architecture to understand how ChatGPT works. The video demonstrates tokenization, batching, loss computation, and text generation using the Tiny Shakespeare dataset, while introducing key concepts like self-attention and efficient matrix operations that form the foundation of modern language models.

directClaims: ChatGPT is a system that allows you to interact with an AI and give it text-based tasks, ChatGPT is a probabilistic system that can generate multiple different outputs for the same prompt, ChatGPT is a language model that models the sequence of words, characters, or tokens, The Transformer architecture comes from the 2017 paper 'Attention is All You Need', GPT stands for 'Generatively Pre-trained Transformer', The Transformer architecture from 2017 took over the rest of AI in the 5 years after its publication, ChatGPT is trained on a good chunk of the internet with pre-training and fine-tuning stages, Tiny Shakespeare is a concatenation of all of Shakespeare's works in a single 1 megabyte file, The character-level language model will predict characters sequentially based on previous context, nanoGPT is a repository of two 300-line files: one defining the GPT model and one training it, The nanoGPT codebase reproduces the performance of GPT-2's smallest 124 million parameter model, Google uses SentencePiece as a subword tokenizer, OpenAI has a library called tiktoken that uses byte pair encoding tokenizer, Block size is the maximum context length for making predictions in a Transformer, A single chunk of nine characters contains eight individual training examples, The Transformer is trained on all context lengths from 1 to block size, Batch size determines how many independent sequences are processed per forward-backward pass, The Bigram language model predicts the next character based only on the identity of the current token, Cross entropy loss measures the quality of predictions against target values, The initial random model achieves a loss of approximately 4.17 for a uniform 65-character vocabulary, Self-attention allows tokens to communicate with their preceding context but not future tokens, A bag-of-words approach averages all preceding tokens to create context-aware representations, Lower triangular matrices can be used with matrix multiplication to efficiently implement causal attention

inferredBeliefs: Language models work by learning statistical patterns in how characters and tokens follow each other, Understanding the fundamentals of neural networks is crucial for grasping how modern AI works, Building simple implementations from scratch is an effective way to understand complex systems, The progression from simple models (Bigram) to complex ones (Transformers) illuminates fundamental principles, Computational efficiency through batching and vectorization is important for practical neural network training, Clear separation of concerns between tokenization, data loading, and model definition improves code quality, Educational value is maximized by implementing models in a modular, step-by-step fashion, Small-scale experiments on toy datasets like Shakespeare can effectively demonstrate large-scale system behavior, Mathematical tricks and efficient implementations are as important as conceptual understanding, Context and history matter: the position of tokens in a sequence influences predictions

mentionedTools: ChatGPT, PyTorch, PyTorch NN module, Google Colab, Jupyter notebook, GitHub, nanoGPT, GPT-2, SentencePiece, tiktoken, Tiny Shakespeare dataset, Adam optimizer, SGD optimizer, CUDA (GPU acceleration), torch.tensor, nn.Embedding, torch.nn.functional.cross_entropy, torch.multinomial, torch.tril, torch.softmax

topics: Download and load raw text data into memory, Build character-to-integer and integer-to-character encoding/decoding functions, Create train/validation splits (e.g., 90/10 ratio), Define a PyTorch neural network module with forward pass and generation capability, Sample random chunks of fixed block size from training data, Create batches by stacking multiple independent chunks together, Forward pass through model to get logits and compute cross-entropy loss, Backward pass to compute gradients, Update model parameters using an optimizer, Evaluate loss on validation set periodically to monitor overfitting, Generate text by iteratively sampling from probability distributions, Start generation with a single seed token and extend sequence token-by-token, Use torch.no_grad() context manager during inference to save memory, Set model to evaluation mode during validation and back to training mode for training, Implement efficient attention using lower triangular masking and matrix multiplication, Use batched matrix operations to process multiple examples in parallel

hi everyone today we are continuing our implementation of makemore now in the last lecture we implemented the bigram language model and we implemented it both using counts and also using a super simple neural network that had a single linear layer now this is the jupyter notebook that we built out last lecture and we saw that the way we approached this is that we looked at only the single previous character and we predicted the distribution for the character that would go next in the sequence and we did that by taking counts and normalizing them into probabilities so that each row here sums to one now this is all well and good if you only have one character of previous context and this works and it's approachable the problem with this model of course is that the predictions from this model are not very good because you only take one character of context so the model didn't produce very name like sounding things now the problem with this approach though is that if we are to take more context into account when predicting the next character in a sequence things quickly blow up and this table the size of this table grows and in fact it grows exponentially with the length of the context because if we only take a single character at a time that's 27 possibilities of context but if we take two characters in the past and try to predict the third one suddenly the number of rows in this matrix you can look at it that way is 27 times 27 so there's 729 possibilities for what could have come in the context if we take three characters as the context suddenly we have 20 000 possibilities of context and so there's just way too many rows of this matrix it's way too few counts for each possibility and the whole thing just kind of explodes and doesn't work very well so that's why today we're going to move on to this bullet point here and we're going to implement a multi-layer perceptron model to predict the next uh character in a sequence and this modeling approach that we're going to adopt follows this paper benguetal 2003 so i have the paper pulled up here now this isn't the very first paper that proposed the use of multiglio perceptrons or neural networks to predict the next character or token in a sequence but it's definitely one that is uh was very influential around that time it is very often cited to stand in for this idea and i think it's a very nice write-up and so this is the paper that we're going to first look at and then implement now this paper has 19 pages so we don't have time to go into the full detail of this paper but i invite you to read it it's very readable interesting and has a lot of interesting ideas in it as well in the introduction they describe the exact same problem i just described and then to address it they propose the following model now keep in mind that we are building a character level language model so we're working on the level of characters in this paper they have a vocabulary of 17 000 possible words and they instead build a word level language model but we're going to still stick with the characters but we'll take the same modeling approach now what they do is basically they propose to take every one of these words seventeen thousand words and they're going to associate to each word a say thirty dimensional feature vector so every word is now embedded into a thirty dimensional space you can think of it that way so we have 17 000 points or vectors in a 30 dimensional space and that's um you might imagine that's very crowded that's a lot of points for a very small space now in the beginning these words are initialized completely randomly so they're spread out at random but then we're going to tune these embeddings of these words using back propagation so during the course of training of this neural network these points or vectors are going to basically move around in this space and you might imagine that for example words that have very similar meanings or that are indeed synonyms of each other might end up in a very similar part of the space and conversely words that mean very different things would go somewhere else in a space now their modeling approach otherwise is identical to ours they are using a multi-layer neural network to predict the next word given the previous words and to train the neural network they are maximizing the log likelihood of the training data just like we did so the modeling approach itself is identical now here they have a concrete example of this intuition why does it work basically suppose that for example you are trying to predict a dog was running in a blank now suppose that the exact phrase a dog was running in a has never occurred in a training data and here you are at sort of test time later when the model is deployed somewhere and it's trying to make a sentence and it's saying a dog was running in a blank and because it's never encountered this exact phrase in the training set you're out of distribution as we say like you don't have fundamentally any reason to suspect what might come next but this approach actually allows you to get around that because maybe you didn't see the exact phrase a dog was running in a something but maybe you've seen similar phrases maybe you've seen the phrase the dog was running in a blank and maybe your network has learned that a and the are like frequently are interchangeable with each other and so maybe it took the embedding for a and the embedding for the and it actually put them like nearby each other in the space and so you can transfer knowledge through that embedding and you can generalize in that way similarly the network could know that cats and dogs are animals and they co-occur in lots of very similar contexts and so even though you haven't seen this exact phrase or if you haven't seen exactly walking or running you can through the embedding space transfer knowledge and you can generalize to novel scenarios so let's now scroll down to the diagram of the neural network they have a nice diagram here and in this example we are taking three previous words and we are trying to predict the fourth word in a sequence now these three previous words as i mentioned uh we have a vocabulary of 17 000 um possible words so every one of these basically basically are the index of the incoming word and because there are 17 000 words this is an integer between 0 and 16999 now there's also a lookup table that they call c this lookup table is a matrix that is 17 000 by say 30. and basically what we're doing here is we're treating this as a lookup table and so every index is plucking out a row of this embedding matrix so that each index is converted to the 30 dimensional vector that corresponds to the embedding vector for that word so here we have the input layer of 30 neurons for three words making up 90 neurons in total and here they're saying that this matrix c is shared across all the words so we're always indexing into the same matrix c over and over um for each one of these words next up is the hidden layer of this neural network the size of this hidden neural layer of this neural net is a hoppy parameter so we use the word hyperparameter when it's kind of like a design choice up to the designer of the neural net and this can be as large as you'd like or as small as you'd like so for example the size could be a hundred and we are going to go over multiple choices of the size of this hidden layer and we're going to evaluate how well they work so say there were 100 neurons here all of them would be fully connected to the 90 words or 90 um numbers that make up these three words so this is a fully connected layer then there's a 10 inch long linearity and then there's this output layer and because there are 17 000 possible words that could come next this layer has 17 000 neurons and all of them are fully connected to all of these neurons in the hidden layer so there's a lot of parameters here because there's a lot of words so most computation is here this is the expensive l

Andrej Karpathy implements a multi-layer perceptron language model as an improvement over the previous bigram model, addressing the exponential growth problem by using learned embeddings and neural networks. He covers embedding lookup tables, forward/backward passes, numerical stability in cross-entropy loss, mini-batch training, and learning rate selection, while emphasizing proper train/validation/test splits to prevent overfitting.

directClaims: The bigram model only takes one character of previous context to predict the next character, The problem with the bigram approach is that if you take more context, the table size grows exponentially with context length, Taking two characters as context gives 27×27=729 possibilities; taking three gives 20,000 possibilities, We implement a multi-layer perceptron model following the Bengio et al. 2003 paper, The paper proposes embedding 17,000 words into a 30-dimensional space, Word embeddings allow the model to generalize to unseen phrases by learning that similar words have nearby embeddings, We initialize embedding lookup table C randomly, then optimize it via backpropagation, The hidden layer size is a hyperparameter that can vary, The output layer has 17,000 neurons for a word-level model (or 27 for character-level), PyTorch's view() operation efficiently reshapes tensors without copying memory by manipulating storage metadata, f.cross_entropy is more efficient than manually computing softmax and log loss, f.cross_entropy prevents numerical overflow by subtracting the max logit value before exponentiating, Mini-batch gradient descent uses approximate gradients on small batches but is more practical than full-batch optimization, Learning rate can be found by searching across exponentially-spaced values and plotting loss curves, The achieved loss of 2.3 surpasses the previous bigram model's 2.45, Datasets should be split into training (80%), validation/dev (10%), and test (10%) splits, The training split optimizes parameters, the dev split optimizes hyperparameters, and the test split evaluates final performance, Neural networks with excessive capacity relative to training data size risk memorizing the training set (overfitting)

inferredBeliefs: Karpathy believes in learning by implementing from first principles rather than using high-level APIs, He values numerical stability and efficiency in deep learning implementations, He emphasizes the importance of understanding tensor internals and memory layouts, He believes systematic hyperparameter search (learning rate range finding) is essential practice, He advocates for proper train/val/test splits as standard practice to prevent overfitting, He values showing common errors and debugging processes in educational content, He believes embeddings are a fundamental mechanism for generalizing across similar inputs, He prefers practical, incremental development over presenting finished code

mentionedTools: PyTorch, Matplotlib, Jupyter notebooks, torch.cat, torch.unbind, torch.view, torch.randint, torch.linspace, torch.arange, torch.range, torch.one_hot, torch.tanh, F.cross_entropy, torch.max, torch.exp, torch.sum

topics: Read dataset of words into a list, Build character-to-integer and integer-to-character mappings, Create training examples by sliding a context window of fixed block size over each word, Initialize embedding lookup table C with random values, Initialize hidden layer weights W1 and biases b1 randomly, Initialize output layer weights W2 and biases b2 randomly, Forward pass: embed inputs → flatten embeddings → apply hidden layer with tanh → apply output layer → compute softmax → compute cross-entropy loss, Zero gradients before each backward pass, Call loss.backward() to compute gradients, Update parameters using learning_rate × gradient descent, Use mini-batches by randomly sampling indices and indexing into dataset, Perform learning rate range search by exponentially spacing candidates and plotting loss vs learning rate, Train for multiple epochs with fixed learning rate, then decay learning rate at end of training, Evaluate loss on full training dataset to monitor actual model performance

This lecture continues implementing neural networks for character-level language modeling, focusing on initialization and internal activation behavior. Karpathy demonstrates how improper initialization leads to vanishing gradients and saturated activations, then introduces Kaiming initialization and batch normalization as solutions to stabilize training across deeper networks.

directClaims: We implemented the multilayer perceptron along the lines of Bengio 2003 for character level language modeling, Recurrent neural networks are universal approximators but are not easily optimizable with first order gradient based techniques, At initialization with 27 characters, we expect a loss of approximately 3.29 (negative log probability of 1/27), The tanh activation function has a derivative of (1 - t²) where t is the output, When tanh outputs are very close to ±1, gradients vanish because (1 - (±1)²) = 0, Dead neurons occur when a neuron always fires in the flat region and never receives gradients, ReLU neurons have completely flat regions below zero that zero out gradients, The proper weight initialization scales by dividing by the square root of fan-in, The Kaiming initialization for ReLU uses a gain of √2, The Kaiming initialization for tanh uses a gain of 5/3, Batch normalization was introduced in 2015 by a team at Google, Batch normalization standardizes activations to have zero mean and unit variance, In batch normalization, scale (gain) is initialized to ones and shift (bias) to zeros

inferredBeliefs: Careful initialization is critical for neural network training, especially in deeper networks, Understanding gradient flow through activations is essential for understanding why certain architectures work or fail, Modern innovations like batch normalization and residual connections have made precise initialization less critical, Visualizing and understanding internal activations and gradients is a core debugging practice for neural networks, The behavior of neurons at initialization significantly impacts training efficiency and final performance, Gradient vanishing problems compound in deeper networks and necessitate architectural innovations, Principled initialization methods based on mathematical analysis are preferable to manual tuning

mentionedTools: PyTorch (torch), matplotlib (plt), torch.no_grad, torch.tanh, torch.nn.init.kaiming_normal_, softmax, ReLU, batch normalization

topics: Initialize neural network weights and biases, Forward pass through embedding, linear layer, tanh activation, and output layer, Calculate loss using cross-entropy (negative log probability), Backward pass through network to compute gradients, Update parameters using stochastic gradient descent, Visualize activation distributions using histograms to diagnose initialization problems, Check for dead neurons by examining columns of saturated activations, Apply batch normalization by computing mean and variance over batch dimension, Scale normalized activations by learnable gain and shift parameters, Evaluate model on train/val/test splits without gradient tracking

This video provides practical guidance on using large language models like ChatGPT, covering model selection, pricing tiers, capabilities including thinking models and web search, and tool use. Karpathy demonstrates real-world examples of how he uses LLMs for coding problems, travel advice, medical questions, and research, while emphasizing the importance of understanding model limitations, managing context windows efficiently, and verifying outputs against primary sources.

directClaims: ChatGPT was developed by OpenAI and deployed in 2022, GPT-4o is the flagship most powerful model currently available from OpenAI as of today, Pre-training stage can cost many tens of millions of dollars and take roughly three months of training, GPT-4o was pre-trained probably many months ago, maybe even a year ago by now, Language models have a knowledge cutoff corresponding to when the model was pre-trained, Post-training stage involves swapping the dataset for conversations built out by humans to attach a 'smiley face' to the model, A one terabyte zip file would correspond to roughly one trillion parameters inside a neural network, Tokens in the context window are expensive in two ways: they can distract the model and they make sampling slower and more expensive, Free tier ChatGPT uses GPT-4o mini, a smaller version with fewer parameters than GPT-4o, ChatGPT Plus ($20/month) users get 80 messages every 3 hours for GPT-4o, ChatGPT Pro ($200/month) provides unlimited access to GPT-4o and other advanced features, Thinking models were only deployed a year or two ago and represent a large breakthrough, Deep Seek was the first to talk about reasoning capabilities in LLMs via reinforcement learning, O1, O3-mini, O3-mini high, and O1-Pro are thinking models from OpenAI, Deep research currently requires $200 per month ChatGPT Pro subscription, Deep research is a combination of internet search and thinking that runs for tens of minutes, Perplexity.com and other services now offer equivalents to ChatGPT's deep research feature

inferredBeliefs: Language models are fundamentally probabilistic and lossy compressions of internet knowledge, Context window management is critical for model performance and cost efficiency, Different LLM providers each have unique strengths and comparing multiple models on the same query is valuable, Models should be evaluated based on their specific capabilities (thinking, search, etc.) rather than treated as universally capable, Thinking models are beneficial for hard problems but wasteful for simple queries like travel advice, Deep research and web search are essential capabilities for models to provide current information, Tool use extends models beyond pure text generation to enable real-world utility, Users should be skeptical of model outputs and verify through primary sources despite sophisticated presentations, The LLM ecosystem is rapidly evolving with new capabilities and models appearing frequently

mentionedTools: ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Grok, Deep Seek, LChat, Mistral, Tiktoken, ChatBot Arena leaderboard, Scale AI leaderboard, OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, Google, Meta, Microsoft, Perplexity, Vercel, Twitter, White Lotus, Wikipedia, WebMD

topics: Start new chat when switching topics to reset context window and improve performance, Check model selection dropdown to ensure using desired model tier, Use search tools for recent information, niche topics, or information that changes over time, Ask multiple LLMs the same question to get consensus advice from an 'LLM Council', Verify model outputs against primary sources before relying on information, Use thinking models for difficult problems in math and coding where extended reasoning helps, Use non-thinking models for quick responses on simple queries, Use deep research for comprehensive reports requiring multiple sources and citations, Check citations provided by models to validate information

This video provides a comprehensive introduction to large language models, explaining how they are built from internet text through tokenization, neural network training, and inference. Karpathy walks through the complete pipeline from data collection and filtering through transformer architecture training to generation, using concrete examples like GPT-2 and Llama 3.1 to illustrate how these systems work.

directClaims: Large language models like ChatGPT are trained through a multi-stage pipeline: pre-training, neural network training, and inference, The first stage of pre-training involves downloading and processing internet text; the Fine Web dataset is approximately 44 terabytes of filtered text representing 15 trillion tokens, Common Crawl has indexed 2.7 billion web pages as of 2024 and serves as a major data source for LLM training, Data filtering stages include URL filtering (removing malware, spam, adult sites), text extraction from HTML, language filtering (Fine Web filters for >65% English), deduplication, and PII removal, Text is converted to tokens using byte pair encoding; GPT-4 uses a vocabulary of 100,277 tokens, Neural networks are trained by taking windows of tokens from datasets and predicting the next token; this is done repeatedly across billions of tokens, Modern transformer models are parameterized mathematical functions with billions of parameters that are adjusted during training to match statistical patterns in training data, GPT-2 (2019) had 1.6 billion parameters, a maximum context length of 1,024 tokens, and was trained on approximately 100 billion tokens; training cost was estimated at $40,000 in 2019, Inference works by feeding token sequences into the network, receiving probability distributions for the next token, sampling from those distributions, and repeating this process to generate text, Base models are token simulators that generate text by continuing sequences from the internet; they are not assistants and cannot answer questions directly, Llama 3.1 405 billion is a base model released by Meta trained on 15 trillion tokens, GPU clusters (like NVIDIA H100s costing $3 per GPU per hour) are essential for training large models; Nvidia's stock value is $3.4 trillion due to demand for GPUs in LLM training, The author reproduced GPT-2 for $600 in one day, compared to $40,000 in 2019, due to better datasets, faster hardware, and improved software

inferredBeliefs: Understanding the mechanics of LLMs is important for proper mental models of what these systems can and cannot do, The internet text that models train on contains biases and inaccuracies that get compressed into model parameters, Scaling up computation and data has been central to LLM improvements; bigger models with more training tokens perform better, Base models have genuine knowledge compressed in their parameters despite not being designed as assistants, The stochastic nature of token sampling is fundamental to model behavior and generates variation in outputs

mentionedTools: ChatGPT, Hugging Face, Fine Web dataset, Common Crawl, Tick Tokenizer, GPT-2, GPT-4, Transformer, lm.c, Lambda (GPU rental service), NVIDIA H100 GPU, Llama 3.1, Hyperbolic (model serving platform), GitHub

topics: Download and filter internet text → tokenize → train neural network on next-token prediction → evaluate with inference, Data preprocessing: URL filtering → HTML text extraction → language filtering → deduplication → PII removal, Training loop: sample token windows from dataset → feed into neural network → get probability predictions → calculate loss → update parameters → repeat, Inference generation: start with prefix tokens → feed to network → sample from output probability distribution → append sampled token → repeat until desired length, Model release: provide source code implementing the transformer architecture + parameter files containing the trained weights

Cloud Code has been shipping features at a ridiculous pace recently. And even if you're not using Cloud Code as your primary driver, it is still fascinating to see how far these tools are pushing the limits for AI coding. Remember, it was only a couple of years ago that we all were using these tools just for autocomplete. And then in the last year especially, we've gotten to autonomous agents that build out entire features end to end. And now Claude Code is leading the charge, turning their coding agent into essentially a multi- aent orchestration platform. It's very powerful with all their recent emphasis on agent teams and sub agents and even coordinating your work across devices. So that's what I want to cover with you right now. All of the biggest new features added to Cloud Code in the last couple of months. And yes, you heard that right, only the last couple of months because that alone already gives us about a dozen features that I want to cover with you right now. like the new 1 million token context window, agent teams, we have get workree support, new commands built right into cloud code. Of course, we have the new remote control. A lot of other ones that we'll hit on very quickly. And the important thing for all of these is you might be using some of these features already. Others you might not have even heard of, but for all of them, I'm also going to give you some tips and tricks for when you want to pay attention to these features and when it might not be important for you, so you can focus on using the parts of the tool that actually matter for you. So with that, let's get into the first feature. First of all, we finally have support now for a 1 million token context window. It is now generally available for both sonnet and opus. And so with this, we can now fit up to 750,000 words into the short-term memory of quad code. So we can theoretically ingest entire code bases. Now, the unfortunate thing with this though, and this is very repeatable with my testing, is once you get to about 250 or 300,000 tokens, you will see a huge uptick in hallucinations. And so, at any point in your conversation, you can run / context to see your current token usage. And so, yeah, it's nice that we can go above 200,000 tokens now, but you don't want to actually push it that much further. Once you get to 250 or 300,000 tokens, you generally want to do a memory compaction or write a handoff prompt and start a new session. So, this is a feature that's really cool to have, but with the limits of the LLMs right now, we don't want to push it too far yet. Next up, we have agent teams. This is similar to sub aents, except these agents that work in parallel can now communicate with each other. So, they can knock off a task list at the same time, and they can make sure that they aren't stepping on each other's toes. And so for example, I can send in a prompt here where I want to create an agent team for a codebase review. So one is going to do a security review, one a performance review, and one a test coverage review. And they might need to communicate with each other because potentially the test coverage review agent might need to understand some of the security concerns that the first one raises. And so we need that real time communication. And if you use T-Max terminals like I am right here, you can see all of the agents working at the exact same time. And I can do controlB and any arrow to switch between the different sessions. So I can work with them in real time. So at any point I can interrupt any of the agents working in parallel on the right hand side here or I can talk to the lead agent and ask about how the agents are coordinating and inject more context at any point. It is very very powerful. I also have a video that I'll link to right here where I cover agent teams in more detail and especially how you can get the most out of using agent teams. Keep in mind that this is a very experimental feature. The agents don't communicate very optimally right now. And so I'd say it's really powerful when you want to build proof of concepts quickly, but I wouldn't use agent teams yet for anything that you're going to be shipping to production. All right, so now we've gotten to actually my mostused new feature for Cloud Code, and that is the native support for Git work trees. And this is a big deal because it allows us to very easily work with multiple copies of our codebase with claude at the exact same time. So I have one workree called feature 1, another called feature 2. And the important thing to see here is that we have a copy of our repo living in the.claude/workreees folder for each of our work trees. And real development is always working with feature branches and different pull requests. So if we want to do parallel work with claude code and we don't want our different feature branches to be overriding each other's changes then we need local copies of our codebase and now we can do that immediately with claude. We don't have to work with separate commands and run claude in different work trees manually like we had to do before. It saves me a ton of time. I'm using this every day. Next up we have a couple of commands that are now built directly into cloud code and these are powerful. So, Enthropic, they were using these internally for a while and they figured like, hey, if this is so good for us, let's just share this with everyone and build it directly into the tool. So, we now have /simplify and slashbatch. And I'll give you a demo of this one in a bit as well. So, slsimplify, you just run this after any implementation and it guides the coding agent on simplifying the codebase because we all know that large language models are way too prone to overengineering and so you end up doing this a lot yourself after anyway. So now it's just a tool built in for us. And then what slashbatch does is this allows us to handle larger changes in parallel. So we're orchestrating large scale changes across our codebase. Any kind of larger refactor is a perfect use case for slash batch because it splits up the work and then dishes out different agents to handle it. There are a million examples where you need to do larger refactors in a codebase. So just to give you a quick example, let's say I have all of these console logs splattered throughout my codebase and I want to replace everything with a more productionready logging strategy. So what I can do is just go into claude and say /batch replace all console log calls with a structure logger that I have already set up in my utilities folder. I can send this off. It's going to create the sub aents, handle all of the coordination itself, and then come back to me with a refactor codebase that can create my poll request. All right, next we have remote control. And this is super cool because it is the coordinating work across devices that I mentioned at the start of the video. What I can do is at any point within a conversation on my desktop here with cloud code, I can do slash remote control. This is going to create a cloud session that I can now use directly on my phone assuming I have the cloud app. So check this out. I'm going to refresh on my phone. I have the conversation here. If I send in a message like hi, then right now we can see it pop up instantaneously on my computer. So I can now work on my computer but from my phone anywhere. Super cool. All right, so for the next couple I'm going to stay pretty high level but they still are very important. Starting with sub aents. Sub aents are becoming a bigger and bigger deal for all AI coding tools right now. There's a video that I'll link to right here where I talk all about this. But yeah, it's just very cool the C-cloud code leaning into building more and more into sub agents. A lot to cover here. You can see the documentation is very extensive. I'll just focus on a couple right now. So, first we have persistent memory. It is not just our primary cloud code agent that

Cole Medin reviews over a dozen new features added to Claude Code in recent months, including 1 million token context windows, agent teams, Git worktree support, and remote control across devices. He emphasizes practical tips for when to use each feature while honestly discussing limitations, such as hallucination risks above 250-300k tokens and the experimental nature of agent teams. The video positions Claude Code as evolving into a multi-agent orchestration platform with powerful new capabilities for parallel work, automation, and memory management.

directClaims: Cloud Code has been shipping features at a ridiculous pace recently, A couple of years ago we were using these tools just for autocomplete, In the last year especially, we've gotten to autonomous agents that build out entire features end to end, Claude Code is leading the charge, turning their coding agent into essentially a multi-agent orchestration platform, The new 1 million token context window is now generally available for both Sonnet and Opus, With the 1 million token context window, we can fit up to 750,000 words into the short-term memory of Claude Code, Once you get to about 250 or 300,000 tokens, you will see a huge uptick in hallucinations, At any point in your conversation, you can run /context to see your current token usage, Agent teams allow agents that work in parallel to communicate with each other, Agent teams is a very experimental feature and agents don't communicate very optimally right now, Agent teams is really powerful when you want to build proof of concepts quickly, but not for anything you're going to ship to production, Git worktrees allows us to very easily work with multiple copies of our codebase with Claude at the exact same time, Worktrees are stored in the .claude/worktrees folder, The /simplify command guides the coding agent on simplifying the codebase, Large language models are way too prone to overengineering, The /batch command allows us to handle larger changes in parallel, Remote control allows coordinating work across devices by creating a cloud session usable on a phone with the Claude app, Sub agents can have persistent memory for future sessions, Hooks can now be defined at the sub agent level for individual agents, AutoMemory allows Claude to curate memories for itself and accumulate knowledge across sessions, AutoMemory is enabled by default, AutoMemory builds up memories in a memory folder within global .claude, Claude.md is the classic rule file with constraints and conventions for the coding agent, The /btw command is for sidecar conversations without bloating the primary context window, Cloud Code does not have tools when in /btw mode, The /loop command allows scheduling a recurring prompt in Cloud Code, Cloud Code finally built voice support into the platform, External speech-to-text tools like Aqua Voice, Whisperflow, and Whisper are still better than the voice mode built into Cloud Code, You can adjust the effort level for models in Cloud Code to balance reasoning capability with token usage, Medium is the default effort level and generally recommended, Opus specifically has max effort which uses a lot of tokens, Scheduled tasks can set one-time reminders and cron jobs for recurring basis tasks, You can manage scheduled tasks with natural language rather than knowing cron syntax

inferredBeliefs: Cole believes AI coding tools have evolved dramatically from simple autocomplete to sophisticated autonomous agents, He values practical, tested advice over theoretical capabilities, Cole is pragmatic about AI limitations and warns against overextending current technology, He sees agent orchestration and multi-agent systems as the future direction of AI coding, Cole believes in giving users granular control while also providing sensible defaults, He values both structured rules (claude.md) and autonomous learning (automemory), Cole appreciates tools that reduce cognitive load and context management burden, He thinks workflow efficiency matters significantly in development practices, Cole appears to favor experimental features for POCs but not production use, He values transparency about experimental features and their limitations

Every six months, software engineering, or at least the coding part of it, is six months from being dead. I am sure you've heard this narrative too many times before. It's a pattern that has repeated itself the last couple of years. Like right here, we have Daario, the CEO of Anthropic, who I I do respect a lot, by the way. But he went on the record March of last year saying that AI would be writing 90% of code in 6 months. And then come 6 months later in September, of course, it is not even close. And then he goes on the record literally just a week ago again saying that software engineers could go extinct this year in 2026 and it's going to be painful for a lot of people. So he said things like this before, but this time guys it's actually going to happen, right? Well, we'll see. We'll see what happens this year. And Daario is far from alone. All of the leaders in the tech industry are saying things like this. Like Jensen Hong saying kids shouldn't even learn to code all the way back in 2024. The Amazon cloud CEO saying most engineers won't even be coding. Of course, we have Sam Alman saying mastering AI tools is the new learn to code. The Microsoft AI CEO even going so far as to say this year that within a year and a half all white collar tasks will be automated. So crazy claims that honestly I think are pretty exaggerated and a lot of these tech leaders are very biased in what they're saying and honestly I think living in a bubble. So right now I just want to have a candid conversation with you. Let's look at the data and think about why are these tech leaders so biased and living in a bubble? What does this mean for us? What's the true power of AI coding assistance? Because there is a lot of stock in what they're saying. But there's just a massive difference between the capabilities of AI coding assistance and true adoption, especially at enterprise level. We'll cover that as well. And I never explicitly ask for comments on a YouTube video. But right now, I am cuz I really want to hear from you what you think about all this and if you think I'm wrong on any account here, like if I'm misinterpreting any data, if your experience is different from mine, cuz we're going to look at the data. I'll talk about my experiences, put that all together, and talk about what that means for us going forward as well. Now, here's the thing. I am not saying that coding agents aren't powerful enough to replace most coding. I've done that for myself. I've built the system. So, coding agents are powerful enough. There is a lot of stock in what these tech leaders are saying, but there are two huge caveats that I feel like they're dismissing way too much. First of all, I've seen firsthand how low the adoption of these tools is at an enterprise level. You would be surprised because I teach both individuals and teams at larger companies how to leverage AI coding assistance effectively. And there is so much resistance in these larger companies that I just think there's no way even within five years most companies will be using AI coding assistance to write 90% of code. And then the other big caveat here is there's so much more to software engineering than just coding. And these skills I think are never going to go away. Maybe you've heard the new term intent engineering showing up on the internet recently. It's really just a lot of software engineering principles put into a package to apply to AI. And people are recognizing that we still need these skills even as coding agents are getting more and more powerful. So I could have made this video even a year ago, but what really put me over the edge to make it right now is a couple of comments that Boris Churnney made in some podcasts that people are taking the wrong way. So he was on the Y Combinator and Lenny podcast. And in both he said that coding is largely solved. Now if you watch both these podcasts, which they are great, and you really get into what Boris is saying, I can get behind it. But when you take this at face value, coding is largely solved. It's a very exaggerated claim that a lot of people are misinterpreting, thinking that software engineering is dying and that he's saying the same thing as Daario. But he literally clarified later on X that software engineering is still very important. He said, and I quote, "Someone has to prompt the clouds. Talk to the customers. This is very important. Coordinate with other teams. Decide what to build next. Engineering is changing and great engineers are more important than ever." And so looking at this right now and Boris, he's the creator of Claude Code. So Daario is his CEO saying something that seems very very contradictory. Software engineering is going to go extinct in 2026. Software engineering is more important than ever. So how do you wrestle with this? I mean these contradictions even within the same company is why I'm very confident in saying that these people are living in bubbles and they're very biased in what they are saying. And all we have to do here is just follow the money. I mean it's a pretty simple argument. Like if we look at Enthropic here, they went from a $61.5 billion valuation to a whopping $380 billion in less than a year. And so yeah, Daario is benefiting a lot when people are leaning more and more into AI coding assistance instead of writing code themselves and trying to be software engineers. So that you have this whole narrative of like don't even get a computer science degree. That's very helpful for people like Dario, also people like Jensen. So yeah, his net worth was only $3 billion in 2019. And then this is pretty outdated, but even in 2024, it was already at $90 billion. Just insane. And this makes sense. Nvidia is selling the chips, most of the chips for LLM training and inference. So, he is also benefiting a lot. And then, yeah, of course, Sam Alman, just as another good example here, OpenAI is valued at over $730 billion now. And by the way, Sam is in line for a 7% equity stake that is going to be worth over $10 billion. So, he's doing pretty good for himself with this whole AI craze right now. So with all of that being said, the truth right now really seems like yes, software engineering is going to evolve significantly over the next couple of years, but it is not going away. Maybe we will need less engineers, but the role is still going to be very important. And also AI is not going to be writing 90% or more of code for most companies. We'll get there eventually, but not like this, not in the next year. Now, allow me to share some of my own personal experience here because I have a lot of it I think will really tie this together because I went from being that engineer that refused to use AI coding tools to now teaching thousands of individuals and teams at large companies how to use them effectively. So, back in 2024, I was one of those people that said no to all AI coding tools. I wrote all of my code by hand still. But finally, November of 2024 is when I installed Windsurf. That was my first AI coding assistant. And man was my brain blown. Like I wish I had used AI coding assistants for the last year. And so I didn't start doing a ton with these AI coding assistants. Like they just help me with writing a function or editing a single file. But over time, like the next 6 months, I built up my full system that I call my piv loop plan, implement, validate. I have a video on my channel where I cover it that I'll link to right here. So, I've gotten to the point now where I delegate all of my coding, at least almost all of my coding to the AI coding assistants because I put myself in the driver seat for the planning and the validation at the end, sandwiching the implementation. So, I'm not vibe coding. I'm still doing everything a software engineer does where I define the architecture and tech stack. I take requirements and translate that into the context for the coding agent. I do a lot of code reviewing and even fixing code by h

Cole Medin addresses exaggerated claims from tech leaders about AI replacing software engineers, examining the gap between tool capability and actual enterprise adoption. He argues that while AI coding assistants are powerful, they require strong engineering fundamentals and context-setting to be effective, and adoption across large companies remains far slower than headlines suggest. Cole presents data showing significant disconnect between enterprise licenses and daily usage, while detailing his own evolution from skeptic to advanced user of AI coding tools through his PIV loop methodology.

directClaims: Dario Amodei said in March of last year that AI would be writing 90% of code in 6 months, but by September it was not even close, Dario Amodei said a week ago that software engineers could go extinct in 2026, Jensen Huang said kids shouldn't learn to code back in 2024, Amazon cloud CEO said most engineers won't be coding, Sam Altman said mastering AI tools is the new learn to code, Microsoft AI CEO said within a year and a half all white collar tasks will be automated, Cole built a system called the PIV loop (plan, implement, validate) for using AI coding assistants, Cole installed Windsurf in November 2024 as his first AI coding assistant, 90% of engineering teams use AI in their workflow according to a 2025 survey, Only 51% of professional developers actually use AI tools daily according to Stack Overflow survey, 76% of executives believe their teams embraced AI while only 52% of engineers say the same, 21% of AI licenses remained underutilized, Boris Churney said 'coding is largely solved' on Y Combinator and Lenny podcasts, Boris Churney clarified on X that 'someone has to prompt the clouds, talk to customers, coordinate with teams, decide what to build next', Anthropic went from $61.5 billion valuation to $380 billion in less than a year, Jensen Huang's net worth was $3 billion in 2019 and $90 billion by 2024, Sam Altman is in line for a 7% equity stake at OpenAI worth over $10 billion, OpenAI is valued at over $730 billion, AI coding assistants still require an insane amount of guidance to work reliably, Enterprise adoption of AI coding tools is very slow due to corporate red tape, procurement processes, and security reviews

inferredBeliefs: Tech leaders are financially incentivized to overstate AI capabilities and threat to jobs, There is a significant gap between the capability of AI tools and their actual adoption in practice, Software engineering will evolve but not disappear in the foreseeable future, Technical skills and understanding of code remain critically important even with AI assistance, Enterprise-level adoption moves much slower than individual adoption, The distinction between owning a tool and actually using it effectively is substantial, Senior engineers' skepticism about AI tools is grounded in legitimate concerns about hallucination and security, Engineering work is increasingly about context-setting and validation rather than just writing code, Engineers who become over-reliant on AI without maintaining deep technical understanding will struggle, AI should be used as a force multiplier, not a replacement for engineering judgment
