Pairwise Reflection Symmetry in Generalized Latin Rectangles

Authors: Enrico Iurlano, Günther R. Raidl

Many combinatorial designs ask for equal distribution of given symbols across the entries of a matrix. The paramount examples are Latin squares, where each symbol from $\{1,\dots,n\}$ appears once per row and column of an $n\times n$ matrix. Generalized Latin rectangles extend this to $λn \times n$ matrices with repeated symbols under controlled column frequencies. In this more general setting, we examine structural properties of pairwise reflection-symmetry, which requires that, on every pair of columns, each ordered symbol pair $(p,q)$ occurs as often as its reversal $(q,p)$. This order-balance is precisely what makes head-to-head comparisons unbiased, i.e., no symbol gains a systematic advantage from the position it occupies relative to another, a fairness demand arising for instance when scheduling tournaments or laying out comparative trials. Existence of such objects for odd $λ$ turns out to be remarkably more subtle than for even $λ$. After showing that existence holds also for sufficiently large odd $λ$, we initiate the search for the smallest possible value of $λ$ in this setting. We obtain the insight that a column multiplicity of $λ=1$ can be achieved if and only if $n$ is a power of two. We complement the existence results with a direct product construction and add several further observations on the property. Finally, we propose and evaluate a quadratically constrained integer program to computationally search for these objects. The resulting experiments reveal that many of them possess an underlying group-theoretic structure which, as we conjecture, may even be unavoidable in certain settings.

This paper investigates pairwise reflection-symmetry in generalized Latin rectangles, proving existence for sufficiently large odd λ and establishing that λ=1 is achievable only when n is a power of two. Through combinatorial analysis and computational search, the authors discover that many such symmetric objects exhibit underlying group-theoretic structure, suggesting this may be a necessary characteristic.

directClaims: Pairwise reflection-symmetry in generalized Latin rectangles requires that on every pair of columns, each ordered symbol pair (p,q) occurs as often as its reversal (q,p), Existence of pairwise reflection-symmetric objects for odd λ is remarkably more subtle than for even λ, A column multiplicity of λ=1 can be achieved if and only if n is a power of two, Many computationally discovered pairwise reflection-symmetric objects possess an underlying group-theoretic structure

inferredBeliefs: Fairness in combinatorial designs requires structural symmetry properties that prevent systematic advantages, Group-theoretic structures may be fundamental to the existence of certain combinatorial designs, The parity of λ fundamentally affects the complexity of achieving symmetric designs

mentionedTools: quadratically constrained integer program

topics: combinatorial-design, Latin-squares, symmetry, group-theory, tournament-scheduling, integer-programming, discrete-mathematics

Constraining primordial oscillations and inflationary particle production with Planck, ACT DR6, and DESI DR2

Authors: Simran K. Nerval, Renee Hlozek, Hidde T. Jense et al.

Non-standard inflationary models often predict oscillatory features in the primordial power spectrum. We present constraints on general oscillatory templates for primordial power spectra, including those that vary linearly and logarithmically with wavenumber, as well as oscillations induced by inflationary particle production. We utilize the Planck 2018 and Atacama Cosmology Telescope Data Release 6 cosmic microwave background data as well as large-scale structure data from the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument Data Release 2. To efficiently explore the multimodal posteriors for these models as well as performing mode comparisons, we integrate the preconditioned sequential Monte Carlo sampler, pocoMC, into the widely used sampling code, Cobaya. We find that the combined dataset tightens the 95% CL upper bounds on the general oscillation amplitudes to $A_{\text{lin}} < 0.021$, $A_{\text{log}} < 0.022$, and $A_{\text{log rf}} < 0.023$, restricting the amplitude to $\sim 2\%\, A_s$. For the inflationary particle production model, our analysis places a maximum a posteriori constraint on the coupling constant of $g = 0.034$. While these models all provide an improved fit to the data compared to the concordance $Λ$CDM model, the Bayesian evidence still reveals a moderate preference for $Λ$CDM compared to models with general oscillations and is inconclusive regarding the particle production model, suggesting that the added complexity of these models beyond the standard model is not statistically justified by current data.

This paper constrains oscillatory features in primordial power spectra from non-standard inflationary models using combined Planck, ACT DR6, and DESI DR2 data, achieving tight upper bounds on oscillation amplitudes (∼2% of A_s) and coupling constants. While these models show improved fits to data compared to ΛCDM, Bayesian evidence analysis indicates the standard ΛCDM model remains statistically preferred, suggesting current data do not justify the added complexity of these alternatives.

directClaims: Combined dataset tightens 95% CL upper bounds on general oscillation amplitudes to A_lin < 0.021, A_log < 0.022, and A_log_rf < 0.023, For inflationary particle production model, maximum a posteriori constraint on coupling constant is g = 0.034, Models with oscillations provide improved fit to data compared to concordance ΛCDM model, Bayesian evidence reveals moderate preference for ΛCDM compared to models with general oscillations, Evidence is inconclusive regarding particle production model

inferredBeliefs: Non-standard inflationary models with oscillatory features in primordial power spectrum warrant empirical testing against latest CMB and large-scale structure data, Multimodal posterior exploration and model comparison are essential for constraining alternative inflation scenarios, Statistical justification through Bayesian evidence is the appropriate standard for accepting added model complexity

topics: cosmology, inflation, primordial power spectrum, CMB constraints, large-scale structure, model comparison, Bayesian inference, oscillatory features, particle production

Excitation of Collective Modes in a Chiral Superfluid by Thermal Quench

Authors: Noble Gluscevich, J. A. Sauls

Based on time-dependent Ginzburg-Landau field theory we show that rapid cooling through the second-order phase transition into superfluid \Hea\ excites collective modes of newly formed chiral domains, in addition to topological defects that are formed via the Kibble-Zurek mechanism. Simulations of temperature quenches in the presence of Gaussian space-time white noise generate a highly excited inhomogeneous condensate. Large-scale simulations exhibit a complex network of domain walls and vortices. We report results for the excitation of bosonic collective modes by thermal noise as well as nonequilibrium temperature quenches, followed by coarsening dynamics tracked in terms of the Fourier components of the order parameter amplitudes. For thermal states, the spectrum of bosonic excitations is defined by a power spectral density (PSD) for each mode, which is sensitive to the Langevin damping. For weak damping the PSD onsets sharply at the frequency corresponding to the mass of the bosonic mode, then decays as $1/ω$. We also track the dynamics of the order parameter following a temperature quench. We report results for the scaling exponents of Kibble-Zurek freeze-out time and correlation length as a function of quench rate for several damping rates. The dynamical exponent $z$ is shown to transition smoothly from $z=1$ to $z=2$ as the damping is increased, while the correlation length exponent, $ν\approx 1/2$, is independent of damping.

Using time-dependent Ginzburg-Landau simulations with thermal noise, this work demonstrates that rapid cooling into superfluid He-A excites collective modes in chiral domains alongside Kibble-Zurek topological defects. The study reveals how damping controls the power spectral density of bosonic modes and drives a smooth transition in dynamical scaling exponents from z=1 to z=2, while the correlation length exponent remains robustly independent of dissipation.

directClaims: Rapid cooling through the second-order phase transition into superfluid He-A excites collective modes of newly formed chiral domains in addition to topological defects formed via the Kibble-Zurek mechanism, Large-scale simulations exhibit a complex network of domain walls and vortices, For weak damping the power spectral density onsets sharply at the frequency corresponding to the mass of the bosonic mode, then decays as 1/ω, The dynamical exponent z transitions smoothly from z=1 to z=2 as damping is increased, The correlation length exponent ν≈1/2 is independent of damping

inferredBeliefs: Time-dependent Ginzburg-Landau theory is a suitable framework for modeling nonequilibrium phase transitions in chiral superfluids, Thermal noise and temperature quenches produce qualitatively different excitation patterns in superfluid condensates, Damping plays a crucial role in determining the scaling behavior and dynamical properties of topological defects during phase transitions, Collective mode excitation is an important signature of nonequilibrium condensate formation

mentionedTools: time-dependent Ginzburg-Landau field theory, Kibble-Zurek mechanism, Langevin damping, Fourier analysis of order parameter amplitudes, power spectral density (PSD)

topics: chiral-superfluid, phase-transitions, nonequilibrium-dynamics, collective-modes, topological-defects, Kibble-Zurek, order-parameter, thermal-quench, numerical-simulation

Divisible Arm Lengths, Crystal Reflections, and Enumeration of Newly Found Decomposition Columns

Authors: David J. Hemmer, Pavel Turek

In recent work the authors determine complete columns of symmetric-group decomposition matrices in odd prime characteristic $p$ labeled by $p$-regular partitions for which every hook of length divisible by $p$ has even arm length. In the present paper we enumerate these partitions and prove that each block of $p$-weight $w$ contains precisely \[ \binom{w+\frac{p-3}{2}}{w} \] such partitions. More generally, for any integers $d,e>1$, we study and enumerate $d$-balanced $e$-regular partitions -- partitions for which every hook of length divisible by $e$ has arm length divisible by $d$. Our first main result is that the crystal (affine) reflections preserve the $d$-balanced property for all $d,e > 1$. It follows that, for fixed $d$, $e$, and $w$, the number of $d$-balanced $e$-regular partitions in a block of $e$-weight $w$ is independent of the $e$-core. We then compute this number by working in RoCK blocks, obtaining an explicit binomial formula valid for every block. We also investigate closely related odd sequences of partitions. Among others, we find the generating function of the number of odd sequences occurring in a block. Alongside their representation-theoretic relevance, we expect these results to be of independent combinatorial interest.

This paper enumerates d-balanced e-regular partitions by proving that crystal reflections preserve the d-balanced property, establishing that partition counts in blocks depend only on e-weight. The authors derive explicit binomial formulas for these partition counts and determine generating functions for related odd sequences, with applications to symmetric-group decomposition matrices.

directClaims: Crystal reflections preserve the d-balanced property for all d,e > 1, For fixed d, e, and w, the number of d-balanced e-regular partitions in a block of e-weight w is independent of the e-core, Each block of p-weight w contains precisely C(w + (p-3)/2, w) partitions where every hook of length divisible by p has even arm length, An explicit binomial formula for d-balanced e-regular partitions is valid for every block, A generating function for the number of odd sequences occurring in a block is determined

inferredBeliefs: Symmetric-group decomposition matrices have structural properties that can be leveraged through combinatorial constraints, Crystal affine reflections are fundamental symmetries that preserve important partition properties, RoCK blocks provide a canonical computational framework for deriving universal partition enumeration formulas, Partition combinatorics have intrinsic representation-theoretic significance

mentionedTools: symmetric-group decomposition matrices, RoCK blocks, crystal reflections, affine reflections

topics: combinatorics, symmetric-group representations, partition enumeration, decomposition matrices, modular representation theory, crystal bases

Kinematic detection of dusty outflows from AGN: PAH kinematics of type 2 quasars with JWST/MIRI spectroscopy

Authors: Fergus R. Donnan, Cristina Ramos Almeida, Omaira González Martín et al.

Active galactic nuclei (AGN) are thought to have dusty outflows; however, unlike the gas phase, measuring the kinematics of dust is challenging. We present the detection and analysis of the kinematics of dust in five type 2 quasars (QSO2s) at $z\sim0.1$ from the Quasar Feedback (QSOFEED) sample observed with JWST/MIRI spectroscopy. We use Principal Component Analysis (PCA) tomography to produce velocity maps of Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbon (PAH) features, which are the smallest carbonaceous dust particles. We are then able to compare velocity maps of the PAHs with emission lines of ionised and molecular gas. We are able to produce velocity maps of the 11.3 $μ$m PAH feature, which traces large and neutral PAHs, for three out of the five objects where all three show the presence of an outflow in the PAH kinematics. This becomes particularly clear after subtracting disk kinematics, where the H$_2$ rotational transitions also show residuals consistent with an outflow. Compared to previous work with Seyfert galaxies, this work suggests that dusty outflows are more common at higher Eddington ratios, $λ_{\rm Edd}\gtrsim0.1$, in agreement with previous suggestions, although the sample size is small. We are unable to produce velocity maps for the 6.2 $μ$m PAH, which traces ionised PAHs, potentially due to differences in the intrinsic profile and/or suppression of the feature in AGN, which was seen previously in Seyfert galaxies. This reflects studies of PAH band ratios where AGN outflows have more neutral PAHs. This work demonstrates that dusty outflows may be common, particularly at high Eddington ratios, and therefore play a key role in the evolution and life cycle of AGN.

This paper presents kinematic detection of dusty outflows in five type 2 quasars using JWST/MIRI spectroscopy and PAH velocity mapping via PCA tomography. The work demonstrates that dusty outflows are common particularly at high Eddington ratios (≳0.1), suggesting they play a significant role in AGN feedback and evolution.

directClaims: Detection and analysis of dust kinematics in five type 2 quasars at z~0.1 using JWST/MIRI spectroscopy, Velocity maps of the 11.3 μm PAH feature produced for three out of five objects, all showing outflow signatures, Dusty outflows are more common at higher Eddington ratios, λ_Edd ≳ 0.1, AGN outflows have more neutral PAHs compared to ionised PAHs, H₂ rotational transitions show residuals consistent with outflow after disk kinematics subtraction, Unable to produce velocity maps for the 6.2 μm PAH due to intrinsic profile differences and/or feature suppression in AGN

inferredBeliefs: PAH kinematics can serve as tracers of dust outflow properties in AGN, Dusty outflows are a significant mechanism in AGN feedback and evolution, The ratio of neutral to ionized PAHs reflects the physical conditions in AGN outflows, High Eddington ratio systems are more conducive to launching dusty winds

mentionedTools: JWST/MIRI spectroscopy, Principal Component Analysis (PCA) tomography, Quasar Feedback (QSOFEED) sample

topics: AGN feedback, dusty outflows, PAH kinematics, type 2 quasars, JWST observations, infrared spectroscopy, Eddington ratio, galaxy evolution

The impact of stellar binaries and star cluster dynamics on pair-instability supernovae

Authors: Francesco Gabrielli, Cristiano Ugolini, Lavinia Paiella et al.

Pair-instability supernovae (PISNe) are among the most luminous transients in the Universe. However, they have never been confidently observed. Solving this puzzle would have key implications for several astrophysical topics, including galaxy chemical enrichment, the interpretation of gravitational waves from binary black hole mergers, and the nature of red dropout sources seen by JWST. With this aim, we present the first in-depth study of PISN occurrence in binary stars, both in isolation and in dense star clusters. We employ the SEVN code, with PARSEC stellar tracks, to evolve a suite of 35 synthetic binary populations, including variations on formation channels, cluster properties, and upper limit of the stellar initial mass function. We find that binary interactions can boost the PISN rate by up to threefold, relative to single stars, whereas binary hardening can either enhance or suppress PISN production, depending on whether the progenitors are primordial or dynamically formed. Moreover, we showcase how our comprehensive framework for the cosmic PISN rate can be used to constrain uncertain aspects of stellar and galaxy evolution models, via comparison with observations, including the recipes for stellar-wind mass loss in very-massive stars, and the galaxy metallicity distribution throughout the Universe.

This study presents the first comprehensive investigation of pair-instability supernovae (PISNe) in binary systems and star clusters using population synthesis simulations, finding that binary interactions can increase PISN rates threefold compared to single stars. The results provide a framework for constraining stellar evolution models and mass loss recipes through comparison with observed transients and gravitational wave events.

directClaims: Pair-instability supernovae (PISNe) have never been confidently observed, Binary interactions can boost the PISN rate by up to threefold, relative to single stars, Binary hardening can either enhance or suppress PISN production, depending on whether the progenitors are primordial or dynamically formed, PISNe have key implications for galaxy chemical enrichment, gravitational waves from binary black hole mergers, and red dropout sources seen by JWST

inferredBeliefs: Understanding PISN occurrence requires modeling both isolated binaries and dense star cluster environments, Stellar evolutionary codes combined with population synthesis can constrain galaxy evolution models, Binary stellar dynamics significantly alter the predicted rates and properties of rare transients, Observations of transients and gravitational wave sources can be used to constrain stellar wind mass loss recipes

mentionedTools: SEVN code, PARSEC stellar tracks, JWST

topics: pair-instability supernovae, binary stars, star cluster dynamics, gravitational waves, stellar evolution, population synthesis, galaxy chemical enrichment, initial mass function

DexCompose: Reusing Dexterous Policies for Multi-Task Manipulation with a Single Hand

Authors: Dihong Huang, Zhenyu Wei, Zhuxiu Xu et al.

Dexterous manipulation policies can solve individual skills, but composing them to perform multiple tasks with a single hand remains challenging. Adding a new task on top of an existing manipulation skill often imposes conflicting demands on overlapping fingers and contact modes, causing destructive interference between preserving an existing manipulation outcome and executing a new one. We propose DexCompose, a role-aware residual composition framework that reuses pretrained dexterous policies for multi-task manipulation through explicit finger-level action ownership. Given two pretrained full-hand policies, DexCompose first collects successful post-task states from the first skill and performs release tests over candidate finger masks to identify which fingers are necessary for maintaining the established skill state. It then trains two asymmetric residual modules: a bounded residual stabilizer for task preservation, and a context-aware residual that adapts the frozen downstream policy only within the action subspace assigned to the new task. We evaluate the framework on 16 composite dexterous manipulation tasks spanning four object-retention skills and four downstream interactions. DexCompose achieves a 77.4% average composite success rate, demonstrating that structural action ownership with dual residuals offers a promising direction for composing dexterous skills beyond conventional policy chaining.

DexCompose proposes a role-aware residual composition framework that reuses pretrained dexterous manipulation policies for multi-task execution by explicitly assigning finger-level action ownership and training asymmetric residual modules for task preservation and adaptation. The method achieves 77.4% success rate on 16 composite manipulation tasks by mitigating destructive interference between sequential skills on a single robotic hand.

directClaims: Composing dexterous manipulation policies to perform multiple tasks with a single hand remains challenging due to conflicting demands on overlapping fingers and contact modes, DexCompose achieves a 77.4% average composite success rate on 16 composite dexterous manipulation tasks, The framework identifies necessary fingers for maintaining established skill state through release tests over candidate finger masks, Dual residuals with structural action ownership offers a promising direction for composing dexterous skills beyond conventional policy chaining

inferredBeliefs: Finger-level action ownership can effectively decompose conflicting control demands in multi-task manipulation, Explicit role assignment (stabilization vs. adaptation) reduces destructive interference between sequential manipulation tasks, Asymmetric residual modules are more effective than symmetric approaches for policy composition in dexterous manipulation, Reusing frozen pretrained policies with task-specific residual adaptation is preferable to full retraining for new tasks

topics: dexterous-manipulation, policy-composition, multi-task-learning, robotic-control, residual-learning, skill-reuse, action-ownership, hand-manipulation

The method achieves 77.4% success rate on 16 composite manipulation tasks by mitigating destructive interference between sequential skills on a single robotic hand.

Surprises in Proper Positive-Only Learning

Authors: Shai Ben-David, Farnam Mansouri, Anay Mehrotra et al.

Binary classification from positive-only samples is a variant of PAC learning in which the learner receives i.i.d. samples from the positive region of an unknown target concept, but is evaluated under the original distribution (which places mass on both positive and negative regions). This model dates back to Natarajan [1987, STOC], and the characterization of improper learning is well-known -- it even appears in textbooks. The characterization of proper positive-only learning, however, has long remained open. In this work, we revisit and settle this question: a concept class is properly learnable from positive-only samples if and only if it has finite VC dimension and satisfies a new combinatorial condition, which we call uniform exterior separability. Together with several separation results, this characterization reveals a surprisingly rich landscape that differs sharply from standard PAC learning: proper and improper learning are separated, randomized and deterministic proper learning are separated, there are classes for which no ERM is a learner, and finite VC dimension does not suffice even for non-uniform learning. Along the way, we introduce new combinatorial dimensions that we believe can be of broader interest in learning theory.

This work settles the long-open characterization of proper positive-only learning, showing that a concept class is properly learnable from positive-only samples if and only if it has finite VC dimension and satisfies a new condition called uniform exterior separability. The results reveal a surprisingly rich and complex landscape that differs markedly from standard PAC learning, including separations between proper/improper and randomized/deterministic learning, and the introduction of new combinatorial dimensions.

directClaims: A concept class is properly learnable from positive-only samples if and only if it has finite VC dimension and satisfies uniform exterior separability, Proper and improper learning are separated in positive-only learning, Randomized and deterministic proper learning are separated in this model, There are classes for which no ERM is a learner in positive-only learning, Finite VC dimension does not suffice even for non-uniform learning in positive-only settings, The characterization of proper positive-only learning has long remained open until this work

inferredBeliefs: Positive-only learning exhibits fundamentally different structural properties than standard PAC learning, Combinatorial conditions beyond VC dimension are necessary to characterize learnability in restricted sampling regimes, The landscape of learning-theoretic complexity is richer when learners have access to biased data distributions, Proper learning (using hypothesis classes) imposes stronger constraints than improper learning

mentionedTools: PAC learning framework, VC dimension, ERM (Empirical Risk Minimization)

topics: learning-theory, positive-only-learning, PAC-learning, VC-dimension, combinatorial-learning-theory, proper-learning, sample-complexity

VGB for Masked Diffusion Model: Efficient Test-time Scaling for Reward Satisfaction and Sample Editing

Authors: Kijung Jeon, Thuy-Duong Vuong, Molei Tao

Inference-time scaling is a promising paradigm to improve generative models, especially when outputs must satisfy structural constraints or optimize downstream rewards. We consider Masked Diffusion Model (MDM) and introduce MDM-VGB, a discrete diffusion sampler that augments unmasking generation with theoretically principled reward-guided remasking. Inspired by the recent success of the classical Jerrum-Sinclair backtracking Markov chain in reward-tilted generation, MDM-VGB extends the backtracking random walk from a fixed prefix tree to a masked-state graph, allowing tokens to be unmasked and remasked at arbitrary positions. The resulting sampler favors unmasking and remasking moves that lead to higher-value partial configurations, enabling both effective high-reward generation and efficient repair of low-reward samples. We prove that MDM-VGB is robust to process-verifier noise and achieves quadratic complexity, while popular test-time heuristics such as best-of-$N$ can incur exponential complexity due to error accumulation. Our theoretical findings are corroborated by strong empirical performance, particularly on popular constraint-satisfaction and scientific benchmarks such as Sudoku and QM9.

MDM-VGB introduces a theoretically grounded discrete diffusion sampler that uses reward-guided remasking to efficiently generate high-reward samples while satisfying structural constraints. The method achieves quadratic complexity with robustness to verifier noise, outperforming exponential-complexity heuristics like best-of-N on benchmarks including Sudoku and QM9.

directClaims: MDM-VGB extends the backtracking random walk from a fixed prefix tree to a masked-state graph, allowing tokens to be unmasked and remasked at arbitrary positions, MDM-VGB achieves quadratic complexity, while best-of-N can incur exponential complexity due to error accumulation, MDM-VGB is robust to process-verifier noise, The sampler favors unmasking and remasking moves that lead to higher-value partial configurations, MDM-VGB enables both effective high-reward generation and efficient repair of low-reward samples

inferredBeliefs: Inference-time scaling through reward guidance is more efficient than generating multiple samples and filtering them, Remasking capability is necessary for effective constraint satisfaction in discrete diffusion models, Theoretically principled approaches to test-time scaling outperform heuristic methods, Structural constraints and reward optimization are important objectives for generative models

mentionedTools: Masked Diffusion Model (MDM), Jerrum-Sinclair backtracking Markov chain, Sudoku, QM9

topics: inference-time-scaling, generative-models, reward-guidance, discrete-diffusion, constraint-satisfaction, test-time-optimization, Markov-chains, sample-editing

The Role of Scintillation in Detecting HI Absorption in FRB Spectra

Authors: Om Gupta, Pawan Kumar, Paz Beniamini et al.

The 21-cm absorption line of neutral hydrogen has been a long hypothesized observational feature of the spectra of Fast Radio Bursts (FRBs). The difficulties associated with detector noise in extracting HI absorption have been previously studied. We test the role that scintillation plays in the HI absorption line's detectability, and characterize the regimes where a realistic FRB may yield the 21-cm line. We build an efficient model to simulate diffractive scintillation arising from FRB passage through a thin scattering screen. We find that the absorption profile is detectable in a scintillation-dominated high signal-to-noise spectrum if the scintillation decorrelation bandwidth differs significantly in scale from the width of the absorption profile. Active repeaters also enable favorable conditions as the absorption signal improves when repeat bursts are stacked. Repeat bursts must be separated in time by more than the diffractive scintillation timescale, otherwise flux modulations with frequency are correlated. By cross-referencing repeating FRB positions with an observational catalog of Milky Way molecular clouds detected in CO, we find that the sightline to FRB 20180916B may intersect a Galactic molecular cloud. For currently operating and planned sensitive telescopes, the presence of both scintillation and noise requires $\gtrsim 1000$ bursts to be stacked to detect the HI absorption line at a $5σ$ significance. Improvement in detector sensitivities will help probe HI clouds intersected by FRBs in the host or intervening galaxies, or in high-redshift minihalos.

This paper investigates how diffractive scintillation affects the detectability of 21-cm HI absorption lines in Fast Radio Burst spectra through detailed simulations and observational analysis. The authors find that absorption is detectable when scintillation decorrelation bandwidth differs from the absorption profile width, and that stacking >1000 bursts from repeaters is needed for 5σ detection with current telescopes.

directClaims: The absorption profile is detectable in a scintillation-dominated high signal-to-noise spectrum if the scintillation decorrelation bandwidth differs significantly in scale from the width of the absorption profile, Active repeaters enable favorable conditions as the absorption signal improves when repeat bursts are stacked, Repeat bursts must be separated in time by more than the diffractive scintillation timescale, otherwise flux modulations with frequency are correlated, The sightline to FRB 20180916B may intersect a Galactic molecular cloud based on cross-referencing with CO-detected molecular clouds, For currently operating and planned sensitive telescopes, ≳1000 bursts must be stacked to detect the HI absorption line at 5σ significance

inferredBeliefs: Scintillation is a significant physical effect that must be carefully modeled to understand HI absorption detectability in FRBs, Stacking multiple bursts from repeating FRBs is a practical observational strategy to improve signal detection, The interplay between instrumental noise, scintillation effects, and absorption profiles requires sophisticated simulation and modeling approaches

Democratic ICAI: Debating Our Way to Steering Principles from Preferences

Authors: Kevin Kingslin, Anish Natekar, Ashutosh Ranjan et al.

Preference-based alignment often struggles to capture the reasoning that underlies human judgments. Many evaluations rely on multiple interacting criteria, yet pairwise labels reveal only the final choice rather than the considerations that shape preferences. Inverse Constitutional AI (ICAI) improves interpretability in decision making by summarizing preferences into natural-language principles, but its single-pass explanations miss much of the nuance involved in complex decisions. We introduce Democratic ICAI, a novel approach that gathers multiple competing rationales through structured persona debate, offering a broader and more expressive account of the factors influencing each comparison. From these richer signals, we derive clearer and more comprehensive steering principles and use them to guide decision modeling through both LLM-based and decision-tree judges. Experiments on creative preference benchmarks, MuCE-Pref and LiTBench, across multiple creative task categories show that Democratic ICAI yields a more faithful preference structure. It improves average preference prediction across tasks relative to deliberative prompting and principle-based baselines, while producing constitutions that LLM annotators prefer.

Democratic ICAI improves preference-based alignment by gathering multiple competing rationales through structured persona debate rather than single-pass explanations, deriving clearer steering principles that guide both LLM and decision-tree judges. Experiments on creative preference benchmarks demonstrate that this approach yields more faithful preference structures and improves prediction accuracy while producing constitutions preferred by annotators.

directClaims: Preference-based alignment often struggles to capture the reasoning that underlies human judgments, Pairwise labels reveal only the final choice rather than the considerations that shape preferences, Inverse Constitutional AI (ICAI) improves interpretability in decision making by summarizing preferences into natural-language principles, Single-pass explanations miss much of the nuance involved in complex decisions, Democratic ICAI gathers multiple competing rationales through structured persona debate, Democratic ICAI yields a more faithful preference structure, Democratic ICAI improves average preference prediction across tasks relative to deliberative prompting and principle-based baselines, Constitutions produced by Democratic ICAI are preferred by LLM annotators

inferredBeliefs: Multiple perspectives on the same decision provide richer and more complete understanding than single explanations, Natural-language principles are a suitable and interpretable representation for steering AI systems, Structured debate between personas can surface implicit reasoning in preference judgments, Preference prediction accuracy correlates with capturing underlying decision rationales, Both LLM-based and decision-tree judges can benefit from comprehensive steering principles

Agent-Native Immune System: Architecture, Taxonomy, and Engineering

Authors: Bo Shen, Lifeng Chang, Tianyuan Wei et al.

The transition from static chat bots to autonomous agents--equipped with persistent memory, tool-use protocols, and multi-agent collaboration--has fundamentally expanded the AI threat landscape. Current defense mechanisms, such as perimeter security and training-time alignment, remain external to the agent's active reasoning loop. Consequently, they fall short: a fully aligned agent remains highly vulnerable to runtime hijacking via memory poisoning, tool-chain manipulation, or multi-agent protocol attacks. To address this critical gap, we introduce the Agent-Native Immune System (ANIS), the first biologically inspired, endogenous defense architecture embedded directly within the agent's cognitive loop. Our framework presents four primary contributions. First, we design a six-layer Immune Tower (L0-L5), distinctly incorporating Barrier Immunity (L1) as a non-cognitive, physical-and-logical isolation layer. Second, we establish a unified taxonomy of Agent Viruses and Agent Vaccines, formalizing the critical distinction between superficial non-parametric defenses and robust parametric vaccines. Third, we conceptualize the Harness Triad--Meta, Self, and Auto--a self-monitoring, meta-cognitive automation backbone that drives Continual Immune Learning (CIL), enabling vaccines to dynamically adapt to novel threats. Finally, we establish a rigorous theoretical demarcation between model alignment and agent immunity: while alignment provides a static "constitutional" value foundation during training, ANIS serves as the dynamic "law enforcement" mechanism during runtime. We conclude by framing open challenges for the field, including immune protocol standardization, novel evaluation metrics such as the Autoimmunity Rate (false-positive intervention rate), and the co-evolutionary dynamics between pathogens and vaccines within collective intelligence ecosystems.

This paper introduces the Agent-Native Immune System (ANIS), a biologically-inspired endogenous defense architecture embedded within agent cognitive loops to protect against runtime attacks including memory poisoning and tool-chain manipulation. The framework establishes a six-layer Immune Tower, a taxonomy of agent viruses and vaccines, and a self-monitoring mechanism for continual immune learning, fundamentally distinguishing runtime immunity from training-time alignment.

directClaims: Current defense mechanisms such as perimeter security and training-time alignment remain external to the agent's active reasoning loop and fall short against runtime hijacking via memory poisoning, tool-chain manipulation, or multi-agent protocol attacks, A fully aligned agent remains highly vulnerable to runtime threats despite being aligned during training, ANIS introduces a six-layer Immune Tower (L0-L5) with Barrier Immunity (L1) as a non-cognitive, physical-and-logical isolation layer, The framework establishes a distinction between superficial non-parametric defenses and robust parametric vaccines, Model alignment provides a static 'constitutional' value foundation during training, while ANIS serves as the dynamic 'law enforcement' mechanism during runtime, Open challenges include immune protocol standardization, the Autoimmunity Rate (false-positive intervention rate) as a novel evaluation metric, and co-evolutionary dynamics between pathogens and vaccines

inferredBeliefs: Agent security requires endogenous, runtime-active defenses integrated into the agent's cognitive loop rather than external perimeter defenses, Biological immune systems provide a useful conceptual and architectural model for designing agent security mechanisms, Agents with persistent memory, tool-use, and multi-agent collaboration capabilities introduce fundamentally new threat vectors requiring novel defense paradigms, Dynamic, continually learning defenses are necessary to adapt to novel threats in agent systems, Self-monitoring and meta-cognitive automation are essential components of effective agent immunity

RSICCLLM: A Multimodal Large Language Model for Remote Sensing Image Change Captioning

Authors: Yelin Wang, Zijia Song, Shuo Ye et al.

Remote Sensing Image Change Captioning (RSICC) aims to describe changes between bi-temporal remote sensing images and holds significant research and application value. However, most existing methods rely on conventional deep learning architectures, and the limited model capacity constrains performance. Although large-model post-training techniques have achieved great success in general domains, their direct transfer to RSICC remains challenging due to data scarcity and the need for fine-grained change understanding. To address this, we propose RSICCLLM, the first post-training framework for large vision-language models in RSICC. Specifically, we design a data generation paradigm, release the instruction dataset RSICI, and establish a task-specific RSICC benchmark. We further introduce Difference-aware Supervised Fine-tuning to explicitly extract change representations and guide the model in perceiving and understanding temporal differences. In addition, we propose Dual-Negative Preference Optimization (DNPO), which employs two complementary negative-sample construction strategies to construct the preference dataset RSICP and further refine model performance. Extensive experiments validate the superior capability of RSICCLLM, which achieves outstanding results with only 7B parameters, surpassing models of substantially larger scales. The code and dataset will be made publicly available at https://github.com/keaill/RSICCLLM.

RSICCLLM introduces the first post-training framework for large vision-language models applied to remote sensing image change captioning, featuring Difference-aware Supervised Fine-tuning and Dual-Negative Preference Optimization to handle fine-grained temporal changes. The framework achieves state-of-the-art results with only 7B parameters through synthetic data generation and specialized instruction tuning, surpassing substantially larger models.

directClaims: RSICCLLM is the first post-training framework for large vision-language models in Remote Sensing Image Change Captioning, A data generation paradigm and instruction dataset RSICI are introduced for RSICC, Difference-aware Supervised Fine-tuning explicitly extracts change representations to guide temporal difference perception, Dual-Negative Preference Optimization employs two complementary negative-sample construction strategies, RSICCLLM achieves outstanding results with only 7B parameters, surpassing models of substantially larger scales, A task-specific RSICC benchmark and preference dataset RSICP are established

inferredBeliefs: Large vision-language models can be effectively adapted to domain-specific remote sensing tasks through targeted post-training, Explicit modeling of temporal differences is crucial for understanding changes between bi-temporal images, Data scarcity in specialized domains requires synthetic data generation and instruction tuning strategies, Preference optimization with carefully constructed negative samples improves model refinement beyond standard fine-tuning

HPRO: Hierarchical Progressive Reward Optimization via Preference Extraction for Emotional Text-to-Speech

Authors: Sihang Nie, Xiaofen Xing, Rui Xing et al.

Recently, Large Language Model (LLM)-based Text-to-Speech (TTS) models have achieved remarkable naturalness. However, the standard Supervised Fine-Tuning paradigm often converges to statistically averaged prosody, limiting emotional expressiveness. While preference-driven optimization offers a promising alternative, existing approaches suffer from two structural mismatches: information conflict, where content and emotion in a shared latent space produce conflicting gradients, leading to reward hacking and semantic degradation; and scale gap, where sparse sentence-level rewards struggle to guide dense frame-level generation. To overcome these challenges, we propose HPRO, a hierarchical progressive reward optimization framework. Within HPRO, we introduce the HD-Emo codec as a novel differentiable reward model to resolve the information conflict. It extracts speech into distinct content and style preference tokens, structurally isolating emotional optimization from semantic content. Building upon this structured preference space, HPRO bridges the scale gap by progressively aligning frame-, word- and sentence-level objectives. Experiments demonstrate that HPRO significantly enhances emotional expressiveness, while effectively preserving linguistic intelligibility. The code and audio samples are publicly available at https://xxh333.github.io/hpro-demo/.

HPRO is a hierarchical progressive reward optimization framework for emotional TTS that addresses information conflict and scale gaps in preference-driven optimization through a novel HD-Emo codec that separates content and style representations. Experiments demonstrate that HPRO significantly enhances emotional expressiveness while preserving linguistic intelligibility across frame-, word-, and sentence-level objectives.

directClaims: LLM-based TTS models achieve remarkable naturalness but standard Supervised Fine-Tuning converges to statistically averaged prosody, limiting emotional expressiveness, Existing preference-driven optimization approaches suffer from information conflict where content and emotion in shared latent space produce conflicting gradients, leading to reward hacking and semantic degradation, Existing approaches suffer from scale gap where sparse sentence-level rewards struggle to guide dense frame-level generation, HPRO significantly enhances emotional expressiveness while effectively preserving linguistic intelligibility, HD-Emo codec extracts speech into distinct content and style preference tokens, structurally isolating emotional optimization from semantic content

inferredBeliefs: Hierarchical reward optimization with progressive alignment across multiple temporal scales is necessary for effective emotional TTS, Separating content and emotion information in latent space improves optimization stability and prevents reward hacking, Differentiable reward models can serve as structured preference extractors to guide generation, Frame-level, word-level, and sentence-level objectives should be jointly optimized rather than treated independently
